items.title: Review Apex, platform API, and developer tooling changes
items.whatChanged: Use the Spring '26 developer guide and release notes to identify new Apex/API behavior, deprecated items, and developer tooling changes before interviews or production rollout.
items.whyMatters: Developers are expected to explain how release notes affect test classes, integrations, deployment pipelines, and platform limits.
items.interviewAngle: Describe your seasonal release review process: read notes, test in sandbox, update automation/code, then document impact.

items.title: Refresh LWC capabilities and UI development guidance
items.whatChanged: Track Lightning Web Components updates, UI API behavior, component platform changes, and developer productivity notes from Spring '26.
items.whyMatters: Frontend Salesforce roles increasingly expect accessible, performant, data-aware LWC implementations.
items.interviewAngle: Explain how you validate LWC changes after seasonal releases using sandbox testing, Jest, regression checks, and UI smoke tests.

items.title: Study Flow Builder and automation release changes
items.whatChanged: Review Flow, automation, and admin-facing Spring '26 updates that affect record-triggered flows, migration decisions, and low-code delivery.
items.whyMatters: Even Salesforce Developers must know when Flow is preferred over Apex and how releases affect automation behavior.
items.interviewAngle: Use a Flow vs Apex answer that includes limits, maintainability, testing, exception handling, and release impact.

items.title: Track security, sharing, and trust-impacting release updates
items.whatChanged: Review Spring '26 changes related to permissions, sharing, user access, identity, session controls, and trusted data handling.
items.whyMatters: Security mistakes are production incidents; premium candidates must connect release notes to secure coding and admin governance.
items.interviewAngle: Explain how you check CRUD/FLS, sharing, user mode, profiles/permission sets, and sandbox regression during release readiness.

items.title: Review API, integration, and event-driven architecture updates
items.whatChanged: Use Spring '26 release sources to check API version changes, integration behavior, eventing updates, and connected app/security implications.
items.whyMatters: Mid-to-senior roles expect integration answers that include auth, retries, versioning, monitoring, and backwards compatibility.
items.interviewAngle: Describe an integration release-readiness checklist: API versions, named credentials, contracts, retries, test data, and monitoring.

items.title: Study Agentforce updates, topics, actions, and grounding patterns
items.whatChanged: Track Agentforce release updates for agent configuration, topics, actions, grounding, testing, monitoring, and safe automation.
items.whyMatters: Agentforce is becoming a major differentiator for senior Salesforce candidates and FDE/AI specialist roles.
items.interviewAngle: Explain how an agent chooses an action, how grounding reduces hallucination, and how you protect data with trust controls.

items.title: Review Data Cloud and Data 360 identity, segmentation, and activation changes
items.whatChanged: Use current Salesforce release sources to track Data Cloud/Data 360 updates around ingestion, data modeling, identity resolution, insights, and activation.
items.whyMatters: Data Cloud is central to AI, personalization, Agentforce grounding, and senior architecture interviews.
items.interviewAngle: Explain DLO, DMO, Unified Individual, identity resolution, activation, and how Data Cloud supports grounded AI.

items.title: Review Admin, Sales Cloud, and Service Cloud usability updates
items.whatChanged: Track setup, UI, automation, reporting, Sales Cloud, and Service Cloud changes that affect daily admin/developer delivery.
items.whyMatters: Junior and hybrid candidates should show that they understand platform configuration, not only code.
items.interviewAngle: Describe how you review admin release notes and decide what to enable, test, document, or postpone.
